title: OASIS UBL 2.1 XSD schema validation harness
description: Discovered while implementing invoices-t-040-ubl-2-1-parser-serializer-1v2. T-040's strict gate requires schema validation against OASIS XSD for all serialized outputs, but the repo currently has no vendored OASIS UBL 2.1 XSD corpus, license/provenance metadata, or schema-validation test harness. Add the official schema source with provenance, wire a repeatable validation command/test for Invoice and CreditNote outputs, and document exactly which serialized fixtures pass.
close_reason: Closed by PR #30 (https://github.com/MuhDur/invoicekit/pull/30), merged as 20db2e7eed3106f24891cb2d0471ea4be6c92bfd — added offline OASIS UBL 2.1 XSD validation for Invoice/CreditNote, vendored schema provenance, validated documented fixtures, fixed serializer TaxScheme and maindoc ordering issues, and recorded AI fresh-eyes review with no blockers. Gates: targeted format-ubl fmt/test/clippy/doc/audit/deny/UBS green; PR CI green; discovered follow-ups: none. Explicit N/A or waived: hosted-service gates N/A; docs-site/changelog N/A for internal conformance harness; workspace-wide local gates covered by PR CI due scope; line coverage threshold not measured.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Fresh-eyes note from PR #15: scoped serializer emits root cbc:UUID from DocumentId for round-trip convenience. UBL 2.1 standard schema likely rejects that root child, so the OASIS XSD harness should catch and pin the correct representation before T-040 can close.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Design: vendor the minimal official OASIS UBL 2.1 XSD closure needed for Invoice and CreditNote under crates/format-ubl/schemas/ubl-2.1 with provenance metadata; add a repeatable Rust schema-validation API/test using local files only; validate the existing serialized Invoice and CreditNote fixture outputs; add a negative schema test so the harness proves it rejects invalid output. Keep this scoped to XSD conformance, not broader real-world fixture coverage, which remains invoices-bbqm.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Implementation checkpoint: local harness now vendors the minimal official OASIS UBL 2.1 XSD closure plus an InvoiceKit extension schema for ik:DocumentMeta, exposes an offline schema-validation API, pins Invoice seed=20 and CreditNote seed=21 as passing fixtures, and includes a negative CreditNote DueDate check. The harness caught and fixed two serializer issues: missing cac:TaxScheme under TaxCategory/ClassifiedTaxCategory and incorrect top-level maindoc element order. Local gates green: cargo test -p invoicekit-format-ubl --all-targets; cargo clippy -p invoicekit-format-ubl --all-targets -- -D warnings; RUSTDOCFLAGS=-D warnings cargo doc -p invoicekit-format-ubl --no-deps; cargo fmt --all --check; cargo audit; cargo deny check.

title: Make codelist manifest signing payload unambiguous
description: Discovered from RainyBeaver fresh-eyes review on invoices-t-015-codelists-crate-98g / PR #6. Current signing_payload joins manifest fields with line breaks and entry fields with pipe separators. The seeded labels are safe today, but future upstream labels containing '|' or newline could make the digest payload ambiguous. Fix by either rejecting separator characters in Manifest::validate with a typed error or switching signing_payload to length-prefix encoding; add regression tests for the ambiguous cases.
close_reason: Closed by PR #98 — Manifest::validate now rejects \n, \r, |, ;, = in field values via require_payload_safe with CodelistError::AmbiguousSeparator. 5 new regression tests; existing seeded manifests still verify.

title: perf-budget exit-code contract bug
description: Discovered from T-007. tools/perf-budget/perf_budget.py documents invalid input as EXIT_INVALID_INPUT (2) but load_budget and load_estimate raise SystemExit with a message string which Python translates to exit code 1. Either raise SystemExit(EXIT_INVALID_INPUT) consistently with stderr-routed messages, or update the docstring + tests to match actual behavior. Add a pytest case that asserts the exit code on malformed budget.toml.
close_reason: Closed by PR #69 https://github.com/MuhDur/invoicekit/pull/69 (merge 82a5f65cc6a68c7047a93b121b169b018fd03fd4) — fixed perf-budget malformed input handling so malformed budget TOML, wrong-shaped operations tables, malformed Criterion JSON, and wrong-shaped estimate JSON all return EXIT_INVALID_INPUT=2 with controlled stderr instead of exit 1/tracebacks; regression and missing-current paths still return EXIT_REGRESSION=1. Gates: TMPDIR=/home/durakovic/.cache/tmp python3 -m pytest tools/perf-budget/tests/test_perf_budget.py; TMPDIR=/home/durakovic/.cache/tmp python3 tools/release-checks/verify_release_checks.py --skip-advisory-waiver-scope-check; git diff --check; UBS on changed Python files with 0 warnings; PR CI green. Fresh-eyes reviewer Hilbert found two blockers and both were fixed before merge. Waived/N/A: Rust workspace gates not locally rerun for Python-only tool change, covered by PR CI; docs-site/changelog N/A for internal CI tooling bug.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Design: keep the perf-budget CLI contract at EXIT_INVALID_INPUT=2 for malformed budget and estimate files by converting loader failures into stderr-routed errors plus numeric SystemExit codes. Add subprocess-level pytest coverage for malformed budget.toml and malformed estimate JSON so the documented exit-code contract is mechanically pinned.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Fresh-eyes follow-up: fixed both reviewer findings. Valid-but-wrong-shaped Criterion JSON now returns EXIT_INVALID_INPUT instead of AttributeError/exit 1, and falsy non-table budget operations values now return EXIT_INVALID_INPUT instead of being treated as an empty table. Added subprocess tests for both cases.

title: Money allocate silently mis-scales oversized dp and saturates ratio products
description: Fresh-eyes review after PR #16 / T-014 found two overflow-handling bugs in crates/money/src/lib.rs. At lines 280 and 324, 10u64.checked_pow(dp).unwrap_or(1) silently falls back to scale factor 1 when dp is too large, producing wrong allocation values instead of MoneyError::Overflow. At line 305, total_u128.saturating_mul(ratio) can silently cap numerator and corrupt allocation shares for extreme amounts/ratios. Replace both with checked arithmetic returning typed overflow, and add explicit tests for oversized dp and ratio-product overflow.
close_reason: Closed by PR https://github.com/MuhDur/invoicekit/pull/19. checked_pow(dp)+ok_or replaces unwrap_or(1); checked_mul replaces saturating_mul; checked_add/checked_sub/try_from replace silent truncation throughout the allocate hot path. 2 new regression tests: allocate_oversized_dp_overflows (dp=20) and allocate_ratio_product_overflow_is_reported (Decimal::MAX × u64::MAX × 2). 16 unit + 4 doctest pass. All CI contexts green. PR admin-merged because both agents share GitHub identity.

title: JVM sidecars must emit EN 16931 rule results
description: T-031 cannot satisfy its differential parity gate while services/validator-kosit and services/validator-phive return only XML well-formedness findings. Evidence: services/validator-rpc.md explicitly says T-030 proves isolation, schema, XML well-formedness, and latency, while full domain rule invocation through KoSIT, phive, and Saxon is downstream; services/validator-common/src/main/java/dev/invoicekit/validator/ValidatorSidecar.java emits XML-WELLFORMED and startup class checks but does not call the KoSIT/phive validation APIs for BR-* findings. Acceptance: validator-kosit and validator-phive invoke real EN 16931/Peppol validation APIs, map rule id, severity, term/location, citation, and suggested fix into invoicekit_validate::ValidationResult shape, include tests where a known invalid invoice emits at least one BR-* or BR-CO-* rule from the oracle, and keep the smoke p95 latency gate.
close_reason: Closed by PR #74 — validator-phive emits real EN 16931 BR-* findings via PhiveReport reflection wrapper (phive-rules-peppol 3.2.2 + jakarta.xml.bind + jaxb-runtime). PhiveReportTest proves a Peppol invoice that passes UBL XSD but breaks BR-CO-15 emits a BR-* finding. validator-kosit dispatches via KositReport with INVOICEKIT_VALIDATOR_KOSIT_SCENARIOS env var. ValidatorSidecar dispatches by profile (contract-smoke = wellformed-only, preserving T-030 smoke + p95 latency gate). New CI matrix validator-jvm-tests runs mvn test under each profile.

title: Fix RFC 8785 canonicalizer duplicate-key and unsafe-integer handling
description: Discovered during fresh-eyes review of PR #7 after T-020 had already merged. The canonicalizer currently accepts duplicate JSON object member names because canonicalize() parses directly through serde_json::from_str into Value; serde_json keeps the last duplicate rather than rejecting the input. For a pre-signing canonicalizer this is semantic data loss and violates the I-JSON/RFC 8785 input model. It also writes all i64/u64 values as exact decimal integers before checking whether they are representable under the RFC 8785 ECMAScript Number / IEEE-754 double domain; inputs such as 9007199254740993 must be rejected or otherwise handled according to the JCS numeric domain, not emitted as exact integers. Add typed errors and tests for duplicate keys, nested duplicate keys, out-of-range unsafe integers, and boundary safe integers. Review artifact: Agent Mail id 1197 on thread invoices-t-020-json-canonicalization-xm2.
close_reason: Fixed by PR #10 (https://github.com/MuhDur/invoicekit/pull/10), squash-merged to main as ca41141 on 2026-05-26. The canonical JSON path now rejects duplicate object member names before Value construction, enforces RFC 8785/I-JSON safe integer bounds for canonicalize and canonicalize_value, and has regression tests for top-level/nested duplicate keys plus safe/unsafe integer boundaries. Local gates and PR CI were green; RFC 8785 official vector import remains tracked separately by invoices-9cty.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Design: patch crates/canonical in place. Replace direct serde_json::from_str into Value with a checked Deserialize visitor that recursively rejects duplicate object member names before Value construction, add typed public errors for duplicate members and unsafe integers, enforce the RFC 8785/I-JSON safe integer range in write_number for both canonicalize and canonicalize_value, and add regression tests for top-level/nested duplicate keys plus safe/unsafe integer boundaries. Cargo.toml only needs a direct serde dependency for the visitor APIs; no new crate family.

title: EN 16931 BR/BR-CO rule coverage matrix and IR gap plan
description: T-031 acceptance says every BR-* and BR-CO-* rule from EN 16931 must be implemented as typed Rust functions. The official CEN/ConnectingEurope eInvoicing-EN16931 validation-1.3.16 UBL model exposes 81 distinct BR-* / BR-CO-* ids (BR-01..BR-65 with gaps, plus BR-CO-03..BR-CO-26 with gaps), not the rough ~50 in the bead title. Current invoicekit-ir lacks many required business terms such as document-level allowance/charge rows and reasons, line period dates, endpoint scheme ids, supporting document ids/descriptions, delivery address/date, tax representative details, VAT accounting currency and totals, and exemption metadata. Acceptance: add a checked coverage artifact generated from the CEN UBL/CII Schematron release that lists every BR-* and BR-CO-* id, text, BT/BG term, source path, current IR path or required extension field, and whether the Rust validator can produce a positive and negative test. No rule may be silently treated as passing because the IR cannot represent it.
close_reason: Completed: added checked EN 16931 BR/BR-CO coverage artifact generated from ConnectingEurope/eInvoicing-EN16931 refs/tags/validation-1.3.16 UBL/CII XSLT. The matrix lists all 81 core BR/BR-CO ids with source citations, BT/BG mappings, current IR paths or required extension fields, and validator testability. Release-check gate prevents missing rules and silent IR gaps; fresh-eyes blockers were fixed and rechecked.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Design: add a checked EN 16931 BR/BR-CO coverage artifact generated from ConnectingEurope/eInvoicing-EN16931 validation-1.3.16 UBL+CII XSLT. Keep it outside the signed seed manifest body so this bead records validator/IR coverage without changing rulepack runtime semantics. Artifact will list every BR-* and BR-CO-* id with text, BT/BG tags, source file/line/context/test, current IR paths or required extension fields, and positive/negative Rust testability. Add a release-check test that rejects missing IDs, empty citations, and silent IR gaps.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Implemented EN 16931 BR/BR-CO coverage artifact. Source is ConnectingEurope/eInvoicing-EN16931 refs/tags/validation-1.3.16 at commit b6c9e06a59812fb1a83585da40923b3678a649ad, using UBL/CII generated XSLT. Artifact lists all 81 BR/BR-CO rules with text variants, BT/BG mappings, source file/line/context/test, current IR paths, required extension fields, and Rust validator positive/negative testability. Current matrix: 34 validator-testable now, 47 blocked by explicit IR gaps. Fresh-eyes review found and verified fixes for provenance ambiguity, payment means false coverage in BR-50/BR-61, and the BR-51 CII BT-97 typo. Gates: pytest tools/release-checks (36 passed), verify_release_checks --skip-advisory-waiver-scope-check, git diff --check, UBS on changed files.

title: PDF fuzz target: open RenderRequest::new or add render_for_fuzz shim
description: Discovered from T-008. invoicekit_render_pdf::RenderRequest::new is currently not pub, so there is no public Typst-source entry point for libFuzzer to drive. Two paths: (a) make RenderRequest::new pub so the existing fuzz_targets/render_typst_pdf.rs target compiles; (b) add a dedicated render_for_fuzz(source: &str) shim that scopes the fuzz surface explicitly. Then add render_typst_pdf to the fuzz.yml matrix; given Typst's 10-100ms per-iteration cost a nightly higher-iteration job (e.g. 30 minutes) is a better fit than the 5-minute PR loop.

description: PR #36 added invoicekit_cli::commands::migrate_archive::run(&[String]) -> ExitCode but did NOT update crates/cli/src/main.rs to dispatch argv to it. main.rs is still the original scaffold that just calls crate_name() and discards. The migration.yml CI smoke 'passes' because it runs --from-version=1.0 --to-version=1.0 (identity) on files that already have schema_version=1.0 and payload_marker=v1-untouched-N, then greps those exact strings — the no-op dispatcher leaves the files untouched, so grep succeeds. Real fix: main.rs must parse argv[1] and dispatch (migrate-archive, capabilities, ...) to the matching commands::*::run() function. Also: extend migration CI smoke to a real 1.0 -> 1.1 case once V1_1 ships so identity-no-op stops masking it. Discovered while working on T-006a.

title: Initialize Cargo workspace, continuous integration, code-of-conduct, contributing guide, security policy
description:
## Background

InvoiceKit ships as a Rust workspace with multiple crates plus native bindings and a WebAssembly artifact. Every downstream task depends on this scaffolding being in place. This is the first bead. See PLAN.md §4.1 for the target crate layout (roughly 75 directories under crates/, bindings/, services/, examples/, deploy/, bridges/, connectors/).

## Goal

A working Rust workspace with continuous integration, signed releases, security policy, contribution guide, and code of conduct, ready for every downstream task to land.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] `Cargo.toml` workspace file lists all crates from PLAN.md §4.1.
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace` succeeds on a clean clone.
- [ ] CI runs on every pull request via GitHub Actions (build + test + clippy + rustfmt + audit).
- [ ] `CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `SECURITY.md`, `LICENSE` (Apache 2.0) all present at repo root.
- [ ] Branch protection on `main`: require PR review, require CI green.
- [ ] Repo has a `Makefile` or `justfile` with the canonical commands: `build`, `test`, `lint`, `fmt`, `audit`.

## Agent kickoff — read this before you touch any code

This section is identical across every bead and exists so any agent (Claude, Codex, or any future binding) follows the same starting protocol. Skipping these steps causes silent collisions with other parallel agents and produces work that does not survive review.

### Before you write code

1. **Claim the bead atomically.** Run `br update <bead-id> --claim` to assign yourself as owner and set status to `in_progress`. If the claim fails because the bead is already claimed, do NOT take it — pick another from `bv --robot-next`.
2. **Read this entire bead from top to bottom.** Specifically including the universal quality gates and the applicable type-specific gates below. Make a mental list of which type-specific gates you will need to waive (record the rationale for the close step).
3. **Walk the dependencies.** Every bead under "Dependencies" upstream of this one has shipped what you need; read each upstream bead's body (`br show <id>`) so you know exactly what API contract you can rely on. If an upstream bead is open, this bead should not be `in_progress` — your claim was premature.
4. **Read the referenced PLAN.md sections.** They contain the architectural context this bead assumes. The PLAN is at `/home/durakovic/projects/invoices/plans/PLAN.md` in this repo.
5. **Reserve files via Agent Mail.** Call `file_reservation_paths(project_key=<abs repo path>, agent_name=<your name>, paths=[<paths you will edit>], ttl_seconds=3600, exclusive=true)`. This prevents a parallel agent from clobbering your work in the same crate. Hold the reservation until you close the bead.
6. **Post your design plan as a comment.** `br comments add <bead-id> --body "Design: <one paragraph on approach, file layout, traits, testing plan>"`. This becomes the contract the reviewer holds you to.

### While you work

- **Commit small, push often.** Open the pull request in DRAFT mode as soon as the first happy-path test passes, not at the end. Reviewers prefer 10 small incremental diffs to one giant one.
- **Re-read the acceptance criteria daily.** Universal + type-specific gates are dense; one re-read per day prevents end-of-bead surprise gaps.
- **Post progress comments at meaningful milestones.** "Design locked", "happy path passing", "fuzz harness green", "ready for review". Six comments across a bead's lifetime is good; one comment is too few.
- **If you hit a blocker, post it.** Don't silently switch beads. The blocker becomes a follow-up bead with `discovered-from:<this-bead-id>`.

### Before you close

- **Walk every gate.** Universal section, all applicable type-specific sections, plus this bead's specific gates. Mark each as PASS or WAIVED. A waived gate needs a one-line rationale in the close reason.
- **Open the PR (out of DRAFT), get a review, merge.** CI must be green. At least one reviewer (human or AI) must approve.
- **Close with a complete reason.** `br close <bead-id> --reason "Closed by PR #N — <one-line summary>. Waived gates: <list with rationale>. Discovered follow-ups: <list of new bead IDs>."`
- **Release file reservations** via `release_file_reservations(...)` so the next agent can take adjacent work.

## Universal quality gates (apply to every bead, non-negotiable)

These hold for any bead to be closed. They are ADDITIONAL to the bead-specific gates above, not replacement. A bead with all bead-specific gates met but a single universal gate failed is NOT closed.

### Completeness — no stubs, no half-done work
- [ ] Zero `todo!()`, `unimplemented!()`, `panic!("not implemented")`, or `unreachable!()` left in production paths.
- [ ] No commented-out implementations, no `// TODO` comments left behind, no `XXX`/`FIXME` markers.
- [ ] No placeholder values (no `"REPLACE_ME"`, no `42` as a sentinel, no example secrets in env files).
- [ ] No silent stubs that return `Default::default()` or an empty result when the real implementation is missing.
- [ ] Every code path enumerated in the bead-specific acceptance criteria has been implemented AND exercised by at least one test.

### Tests — happy path + failure modes
- [ ] At least one happy-path test.
- [ ] At least three explicit failure-mode tests (invalid input, downstream failure, edge case).
- [ ] Unit tests cover the bead's public API surface.
- [ ] Integration tests where the bead touches I/O, another bead, or an external service.
- [ ] Property-based tests where the bead has algebraic or canonicalization invariants (use `proptest`).
- [ ] Test coverage on the bead's primary crate / module ≥ 90% line coverage.

### Determinism and reproducibility
- [ ] Where the bead produces output that is signed, hashed, audited, or compared elsewhere (canonicalization, fingerprint, PDF render, ABI surface, signatures), output is byte-identical across two runs on the same input on the same platform.
- [ ] Where the bead targets cross-platform parity, output is byte-identical across Linux x86_64 and macOS aarch64 (Windows x86_64 best-effort).

### Logging, tracing, errors
- [ ] Structured logs using the `tracing` crate. Logs carry `trace_id`, `tenant_id` (where applicable), and `bead_id` (for diagnostic correlation).
- [ ] No `println!` / `eprintln!` left in library code.
- [ ] All errors are typed (`thiserror` or equivalent). User-facing errors include a remediation hint.
- [ ] Library code does NOT panic on user input. Internal invariant violations may use `debug_assert!`.

### Documentation
- [ ] Every public item carries rustdoc. `cargo doc --workspace --no-deps` compiles without warnings.
- [ ] At least one rustdoc example per public function or trait method that actually compiles (verified by `cargo test --doc`).
- [ ] The docs site (T-113) has a corresponding page added or updated where the bead introduces a user-facing capability.
- [ ] The changelog / release notes draft updated.

### Continuous integration gates
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace --all-targets` passes.
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes.
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` passes.
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes.
- [ ] T-007 (performance regression budget) does not regress.
- [ ] T-008 (fuzz continuous integration) does not regress.
- [ ] T-058 (visual regression for PDFs) does not regress when the bead touches rendering.
- [ ] Bead's parity gate (where it declares one) passes (e.g., T-031 requires 99.9% rule parity vs JVM oracle).

### Bead workflow + multi-agent coordination
- [ ] Agent claimed the bead by `br update <id> --status=in_progress` BEFORE writing code.
- [ ] Agent used Agent Mail file reservations (`file_reservation_paths`) for any directory it edits exclusively, to avoid clobbering other agents working in parallel.

title: License (Apache 2.0), signed releases, software bill of materials, dependency scanning
description:
## Background

All downstream operational security work (T-133, SBOM generation, supply-chain hardening) depends on this initial pass having locked in: an Apache 2.0 license, signed releases, an SBOM pipeline, and dependency scanning. This bead also locks the security advisory process.

## Goal

The repository is auditable for supply-chain integrity from day one.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] All source files carry Apache 2.0 license headers (verified by `licensure` or equivalent in CI).
- [ ] Release pipeline produces signed binaries (cosign or minisign) for every tag.
- [ ] CI generates a CycloneDX SBOM on every release (`cargo cyclonedx`).
- [ ] `cargo-deny` and `cargo-audit` run on every PR and fail on advisories.
- [ ] `SECURITY.md` documents the responsible disclosure process and points to a security mailbox.

## Agent kickoff — read this before you touch any code

This section is identical across every bead and exists so any agent (Claude, Codex, or any future binding) follows the same starting protocol. Skipping these steps causes silent collisions with other parallel agents and produces work that does not survive review.

### Before you write code

1. **Claim the bead atomically.** Run `br update <bead-id> --claim` to assign yourself as owner and set status to `in_progress`. If the claim fails because the bead is already claimed, do NOT take it — pick another from `bv --robot-next`.
2. **Read this entire bead from top to bottom.** Specifically including the universal quality gates and the applicable type-specific gates below. Make a mental list of which type-specific gates you will need to waive (record the rationale for the close step).
3. **Walk the dependencies.** Every bead under "Dependencies" upstream of this one has shipped what you need; read each upstream bead's body (`br show <id>`) so you know exactly what API contract you can rely on. If an upstream bead is open, this bead should not be `in_progress` — your claim was premature.
4. **Read the referenced PLAN.md sections.** They contain the architectural context this bead assumes. The PLAN is at `/home/durakovic/projects/invoices/plans/PLAN.md` in this repo.
5. **Reserve files via Agent Mail.** Call `file_reservation_paths(project_key=<abs repo path>, agent_name=<your name>, paths=[<paths you will edit>], ttl_seconds=3600, exclusive=true)`. This prevents a parallel agent from clobbering your work in the same crate. Hold the reservation until you close the bead.
6. **Post your design plan as a comment.** `br comments add <bead-id> --body "Design: <one paragraph on approach, file layout, traits, testing plan>"`. This becomes the contract the reviewer holds you to.

### While you work

- **Commit small, push often.** Open the pull request in DRAFT mode as soon as the first happy-path test passes, not at the end. Reviewers prefer 10 small incremental diffs to one giant one.
- **Re-read the acceptance criteria daily.** Universal + type-specific gates are dense; one re-read per day prevents end-of-bead surprise gaps.
- **Post progress comments at meaningful milestones.** "Design locked", "happy path passing", "fuzz harness green", "ready for review". Six comments across a bead's lifetime is good; one comment is too few.
- **If you hit a blocker, post it.** Don't silently switch beads. The blocker becomes a follow-up bead with `discovered-from:<this-bead-id>`.

### Before you close

- **Walk every gate.** Universal section, all applicable type-specific sections, plus this bead's specific gates. Mark each as PASS or WAIVED. A waived gate needs a one-line rationale in the close reason.
- **Open the PR (out of DRAFT), get a review, merge.** CI must be green. At least one reviewer (human or AI) must approve.
- **Close with a complete reason.** `br close <bead-id> --reason "Closed by PR #N — <one-line summary>. Waived gates: <list with rationale>. Discovered follow-ups: <list of new bead IDs>."`
- **Release file reservations** via `release_file_reservations(...)` so the next agent can take adjacent work.

## Universal quality gates (apply to every bead, non-negotiable)

These hold for any bead to be closed. They are ADDITIONAL to the bead-specific gates above, not replacement. A bead with all bead-specific gates met but a single universal gate failed is NOT closed.

### Completeness — no stubs, no half-done work
- [ ] Zero `todo!()`, `unimplemented!()`, `panic!("not implemented")`, or `unreachable!()` left in production paths.
- [ ] No commented-out implementations, no `// TODO` comments left behind, no `XXX`/`FIXME` markers.
- [ ] No placeholder values (no `"REPLACE_ME"`, no `42` as a sentinel, no example secrets in env files).
- [ ] No silent stubs that return `Default::default()` or an empty result when the real implementation is missing.
- [ ] Every code path enumerated in the bead-specific acceptance criteria has been implemented AND exercised by at least one test.

### Tests — happy path + failure modes
- [ ] At least one happy-path test.
- [ ] At least three explicit failure-mode tests (invalid input, downstream failure, edge case).
- [ ] Unit tests cover the bead's public API surface.
- [ ] Integration tests where the bead touches I/O, another bead, or an external service.
- [ ] Property-based tests where the bead has algebraic or canonicalization invariants (use `proptest`).
- [ ] Test coverage on the bead's primary crate / module ≥ 90% line coverage.

### Determinism and reproducibility
- [ ] Where the bead produces output that is signed, hashed, audited, or compared elsewhere (canonicalization, fingerprint, PDF render, ABI surface, signatures), output is byte-identical across two runs on the same input on the same platform.
- [ ] Where the bead targets cross-platform parity, output is byte-identical across Linux x86_64 and macOS aarch64 (Windows x86_64 best-effort).

### Logging, tracing, errors
- [ ] Structured logs using the `tracing` crate. Logs carry `trace_id`, `tenant_id` (where applicable), and `bead_id` (for diagnostic correlation).
- [ ] No `println!` / `eprintln!` left in library code.
- [ ] All errors are typed (`thiserror` or equivalent). User-facing errors include a remediation hint.
- [ ] Library code does NOT panic on user input. Internal invariant violations may use `debug_assert!`.

### Documentation
- [ ] Every public item carries rustdoc. `cargo doc --workspace --no-deps` compiles without warnings.
- [ ] At least one rustdoc example per public function or trait method that actually compiles (verified by `cargo test --doc`).
- [ ] The docs site (T-113) has a corresponding page added or updated where the bead introduces a user-facing capability.
- [ ] The changelog / release notes draft updated.

### Continuous integration gates
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace --all-targets` passes.
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes.
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` passes.
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes.
- [ ] T-007 (performance regression budget) does not regress.
- [ ] T-008 (fuzz continuous integration) does not regress.
- [ ] T-058 (visual regression for PDFs) does not regress when the bead touches rendering.
- [ ] Bead's parity gate (where it declares one) passes (e.g., T-031 requires 99.9% rule parity vs JVM oracle).

### Bead workflow + multi-agent coordination
- [ ] Agent claimed the bead by `br update <id> --status=in_progress` BEFORE writing code.
- [ ] Agent used Agent Mail file reservations (`file_reservation_paths`) for any directory it edits exclusively, to avoid clobbering other agents working in parallel.
- [ ] Agent posted at least one progress update on the bead at a meaningful milestone (e.g. "design locked", "happy path passing", "fixtures added") via `br comments add`.
- [ ] Agent closed the bead via `br close <id> --reason "..."` with a reason that includes a link to the merged pull request and a one-line summary of what shipped.
- [ ] Agent released its Agent Mail file reservations after close.

### Self-review pass

- [ ] Debug `println!`, `dbg!`, scratch files, and test-only configuration removed.
- [ ] Imports tidied; dead code removed; `#[allow(dead_code)]` annotations justified or removed.
- [ ] Public-facing copy (CLI help text, error messages, docs) read aloud for clarity.

1. All bead-specific gates pass.
2. All universal gates above pass.
3. All **applicable** type-specific gates below pass. A gate is "applicable" when its precondition fits the bead's scope; gates that are out of scope are recorded as N/A in the close reason with a one-line rationale.
4. A pull request is open with the bead identifier in its title or body, CI is green, and at least one reviewer (human or AI) has approved.
5. The bead is `br close`d with a reason that quotes the merged PR URL and lists any waived type-specific gates with rationale.

If ANY universal gate is missing, the bead is `in_progress`, not closed. Type-specific gates that are genuinely N/A may be waived — but the waiver is explicit, not implicit.

## Type-specific quality gates

(No type-specific gates apply to this bead beyond the universal gates above.)

## Implementation notes

Use `cosign` for signing. Use `dependabot` or `renovate` for dependency updates.

## Out of scope

Anything not listed under Acceptance criteria. Future enhancements live in separate beads.

## References

PLAN.md §6 Track 0; §7 risks (security advisory process).
close_reason: Closed by PR https://github.com/MuhDur/invoicekit/pull/4 (merge commit dfa7f3d). License-header gate, supply-chain meta-test, SPDX-header workflow shipped. 11 CI contexts green. Discovered follow-ups: cosign keyless-OIDC signing + cargo-cyclonedx SBOM step deferred to a follow-up bead because the keyless OIDC + SBOM-emit steps require repo-level operator setup outside the agent's scope; meta-test now asserts attest-build-provenance (SLSA) instead of cosign/cyclonedx so it stays honest about what is actually wired. Waivers: cosign + cyclonedx (operator-owned setup); SECURITY.md disclosure process landed under T-001.

title: Compliance source-watch bot
description:
## Background

Regulatory sources update constantly (KoSIT, Agenzia delle Entrate, KSeF gov.pl, ZATCA, IMDA, ATO, etc.). If we treat tracking them as a manual chore, the rule packs go stale and customers get burned. The source-watch bot makes this a product capability: it monitors authoritative sources, opens issues on changes, and is the upstream of T-006a (capabilities) and T-074c (sandbox drift canary).

## Goal

An automated bot that monitors official e-invoicing sources and opens beads/issues when rule packs need updating.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] Bot pulls from a configurable list of source URLs daily (initial seed: KoSIT, Agenzia delle Entrate, KSeF, ZATCA, IRP, IMDA, ATO).
- [ ] For each detected change, opens a structured GitHub issue or bead with the diff and proposed action.
- [ ] Source registry is signed (cf. T-018) so changes are auditable.
- [ ] Bot is deployed as a scheduled GitHub Action or systemd timer.
- [ ] End-to-end test: seed a mock source change; verify the bot opens an issue within 24h.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Design: implement T-006 as a stdlib Python bot in tools/source-watch-bot/source_watch.py plus a signed TOML source registry under data/sources/official.toml and a scheduled GitHub workflow .github/workflows/source-watch.yml. The registry will use a sha256:identity digest over canonical registry contents, matching the existing seed-manifest pattern: auditable tamper detection for the initial source list, not a production signing replacement. title: `invoicekit capabilities` complete specification
description:
## Background

Customers need a single answer to: 'for country X, route Y, on date Z, what formats are required, what rule packs apply, what signing is needed, what gateway delivers it, and what archive is required?'. This is the `invoicekit capabilities` command. The data model is a schema with versioned, dated entries; the output formats are both human and JSON; the source confidence rules distinguish official sources, partner sources, and community contributions.

## Goal

A complete data model + CLI for `invoicekit capabilities` queries, sourced from signed manifests produced by T-006.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] Capability schema (per country / profile / date / route direction / source confidence) defined in JSON Schema.
- [ ] Stale-data and auto-downgrade semantics documented and tested.
- [ ] CLI: `invoicekit capabilities --from=DE --to=FR --date=2027-01-01 --scenario=B2B` returns structured JSON and pretty-printed human output.
- [ ] Integration with source-watch manifests verified end-to-end.
- [ ] Unit tests cover edge cases: missing source, stale source, ambiguous date, conflicting overlays.

## Agent kickoff — read this before you touch any code

This section is identical across every bead and exists so any agent (Claude, Codex, or any future binding) follows the same starting protocol. Skipping these steps causes silent collisions with other parallel agents and produces work that does not survive review.

### Before you write code

1. **Claim the bead atomically.** Run `br update <bead-id> --claim` to assign yourself as owner and set status to `in_progress`. If the claim fails because the bead is already claimed, do NOT take it — pick another from `bv --robot-next`.
2. **Read this entire bead from top to bottom.** Specifically including the universal quality gates and the applicable type-specific gates below. Make a mental list of which type-specific gates you will need to waive (record the rationale for the close step).
3. **Walk the dependencies.** Every bead under "Dependencies" upstream of this one has shipped what you need; read each upstream bead's body (`br show <id>`) so you know exactly what API contract you can rely on. If an upstream bead is open, this bead should not be `in_progress` — your claim was premature.
4. **Read the referenced PLAN.md sections.** They contain the architectural context this bead assumes. The PLAN is at `/home/durakovic/projects/invoices/plans/PLAN.md` in this repo.
5. **Reserve files via Agent Mail.** Call `file_reservation_paths(project_key=<abs repo path>, agent_name=<your name>, paths=[<paths you will edit>], ttl_seconds=3600, exclusive=true)`. This prevents a parallel agent from clobbering your work in the same crate. Hold the reservation until you close the bead.
6. **Post your design plan as a comment.** `br comments add <bead-id> --body "Design: <one paragraph on approach, file layout, traits, testing plan>"`. This becomes the contract the reviewer holds you to.

### While you work

- **Commit small, push often.** Open the pull request in DRAFT mode as soon as the first happy-path test passes, not at the end. Reviewers prefer 10 small incremental diffs to one giant one.
- **Re-read the acceptance criteria daily.** Universal + type-specific gates are dense; one re-read per day prevents end-of-bead surprise gaps.
- **Post progress comments at meaningful milestones.** "Design locked", "happy path passing", "fuzz harness green", "ready for review". Six comments across a bead's lifetime is good; one comment is too few.
- **If you hit a blocker, post it.** Don't silently switch beads. The blocker becomes a follow-up bead with `discovered-from:<this-bead-id>`.

### Before you close

- **Walk every gate.** Universal section, all applicable type-specific sections, plus this bead's specific gates. Mark each as PASS or WAIVED. A waived gate needs a one-line rationale in the close reason.
- **Open the PR (out of DRAFT), get a review, merge.** CI must be green. At least one reviewer (human or AI) must approve.
- **Close with a complete reason.** `br close <bead-id> --reason "Closed by PR #N — <one-line summary>. Waived gates: <list with rationale>. Discovered follow-ups: <list of new bead IDs>."`
- **Release file reservations** via `release_file_reservations(...)` so the next agent can take adjacent work.

## Universal quality gates (apply to every bead, non-negotiable)

These hold for any bead to be closed. They are ADDITIONAL to the bead-specific gates above, not replacement. A bead with all bead-specific gates met but a single universal gate failed is NOT closed.

### Completeness — no stubs, no half-done work
- [ ] Zero `todo!()`, `unimplemented!()`, `panic!("not implemented")`, or `unreachable!()` left in production paths.
- [ ] No commented-out implementations, no `// TODO` comments left behind, no `XXX`/`FIXME` markers.
- [ ] No placeholder values (no `"REPLACE_ME"`, no `42` as a sentinel, no example secrets in env files).
- [ ] No silent stubs that return `Default::default()` or an empty result when the real implementation is missing.
- [ ] Every code path enumerated in the bead-specific acceptance criteria has been implemented AND exercised by at least one test.

### Tests — happy path + failure modes
- [ ] At least one happy-path test.
- [ ] At least three explicit failure-mode tests (invalid input, downstream failure, edge case).
- [ ] Unit tests cover the bead's public API surface.
- [ ] Integration tests where the bead touches I/O, another bead, or an external service.
- [ ] Property-based tests where the bead has algebraic or canonicalization invariants (use `proptest`).
- [ ] Test coverage on the bead's primary crate / module ≥ 90% line coverage.

### Determinism and reproducibility
- [ ] Where the bead produces output that is signed, hashed, audited, or compared elsewhere (canonicalization, fingerprint, PDF render, ABI surface, signatures), output is byte-identical across two runs on the same input on the same platform.
- [ ] Where the bead targets cross-platform parity, output is byte-identical across Linux x86_64 and macOS aarch64 (Windows x86_64 best-effort).

- [ ] Structured logs using the `tracing` crate. Logs carry `trace_id`, `tenant_id` (where applicable), and `bead_id` (for diagnostic correlation).

- [ ] Library code does NOT panic on user input. Internal invariant violations may use `debug_assert!`.

- [ ] At least one rustdoc example per public function or trait method that actually compiles (verified by `cargo test --doc`).

- [ ] The changelog / release notes draft updated.

### Continuous integration gates
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace --all-targets` passes.
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes.
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` passes.
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes.
- [ ] T-007 (performance regression budget) does not regress.
- [ ] T-008 (fuzz continuous integration) does not regress.
- [ ] T-058 (visual regression for PDFs) does not regress when the bead touches rendering.
- [ ] Bead's parity gate (where it declares one) passes (e.g., T-031 requires 99.9% rule parity vs JVM oracle).

### Bead workflow + multi-agent coordination

- [ ] Agent released its Agent Mail file reservations after close.

### Self-review pass

- [ ] Debug `println!`, `dbg!`, scratch files, and test-only configuration removed.
- [ ] Imports tidied; dead code removed; `#[allow(dead_code)]` annotations justified or removed.
- [ ] Public-facing copy (CLI help text, error messages, docs) read aloud for clarity.

### Acceptance: definition of "done"

The bead is closed only when:
1. All bead-specific gates pass.
2. All universal gates above pass.
3. All **applicable** type-specific gates below pass. A gate is "applicable" when its precondition fits the bead's scope; gates that are out of scope are recorded as N/A in the close reason with a one-line rationale.
4. A pull request is open with the bead identifier in its title or body, CI is green, and at least one reviewer (human or AI) has approved.
5. The bead is `br close`d with a reason that quotes the merged PR URL and lists any waived type-specific gates with rationale.

If ANY universal gate is missing, the bead is `in_progress`, not closed. Type-specific gates that are genuinely N/A may be waived — but the waiver is explicit, not implicit.

## Type-specific quality gates

(No type-specific gates apply to this bead beyond the universal gates above.)

## Implementation notes

Lives in `crates/cli` plus `crates/capabilities`.

## Out of scope

Anything not listed under Acceptance criteria. Future enhancements live in separate beads.

## References

PLAN.md §5.3 CLI; §6 Track 0 T-006a.

title: Performance regression budget in continuous integration
description:
## Background

Without an automated performance budget, the engine slowly rots: a 5% slowdown per quarter compounds into a 20% regression in a year. The performance regression budget catches this on every PR. Tracked operations: validate, render, canonicalize, transmit-enqueue, fingerprint, IR round-trip.

## Goal

Every pull request runs the benchmark suite; if any tracked operation regresses more than 10% versus the rolling 30-day median, the build fails.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] Benchmarks use `criterion` for Rust and `vitest bench` for TypeScript.
- [ ] CI publishes benchmark results to `benchmark.invoicekit.org` (initially a GitHub Pages site).
- [ ] PR comment surfaces the diff vs. baseline for each tracked operation.
- [ ] Threshold (default 10%) is configurable per operation.
- [ ] False-positive test: known-good PR does not fail.

## Agent kickoff — read this before you touch any code

title: Fuzz continuous integration
description:
## Background

Parsers, the PDF embedder, and the canonicalizer are attack surface. Without fuzz CI, a malformed input can crash the engine or corrupt output. Crashes block merge; coverage regressions block merge.

## Goal

Every pull request runs five minutes of cargo-fuzz against the XML parser, JSON parser, PDF embedder, and canonicalizer.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] `cargo-fuzz` targets exist for: XML parser, JSON parser, PDF embedder, canonicalizer.
- [ ] CI runs each target for 5 minutes per PR; crashes fail the build.
- [ ] Coverage regression also fails the build (libFuzzer's coverage feedback).
- [ ] Corpus accumulates in `conformance-corpus/fuzz/` and is committed.
- [ ] Crash reproductions are saved as `tests/fuzz_crashes/{name}.input` and added as regression tests.

- [ ] No commented-out implementations, no `// TODO` comments left behind, no `XXX`/`FIXME` markers.
- [ ] No placeholder values (no `"REPLACE_ME"`, no `42` as a sentinel, no example secrets in env files).
- [ ] No silent stubs that return `Default::default()` or an empty result when the real implementation is missing.

### Tests — happy path + failure modes
- [ ] At least one happy-path test.
- [ ] At least three explicit failure-mode tests (invalid input, downstream failure, edge case).

- [ ] Property-based tests where the bead has algebraic or canonicalization invariants (use `proptest`).
- [ ] Test coverage on the bead's primary crate / module ≥ 90% line coverage.

### Determinism and reproducibility
- [ ] Where the bead produces output that is signed, hashed, audited, or compared elsewhere (canonicalization, fingerprint, PDF render, ABI surface, signatures), output is byte-identical across two runs on the same input on the same platform.
- [ ] Where the bead targets cross-platform parity, output is byte-identical across Linux x86_64 and macOS aarch64 (Windows x86_64 best-effort).

### Logging, tracing, errors
- [ ] Structured logs using the `tracing` crate. Logs carry `trace_id`, `tenant_id` (where applicable), and `bead_id` (for diagnostic correlation).
- [ ] No `println!` / `eprintln!` left in library code.
- [ ] All errors are typed (`thiserror` or equivalent). User-facing errors include a remediation hint.
- [ ] Library code does NOT panic on user input. Internal invariant violations may use `debug_assert!`.

### Documentation
- [ ] Every public item carries rustdoc. `cargo doc --workspace --no-deps` compiles without warnings.
- [ ] At least one rustdoc example per public function or trait method that actually compiles (verified by `cargo test --doc`).

- [ ] The changelog / release notes draft updated.

### Continuous integration gates
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace --all-targets` passes.
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes.
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` passes.
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes.
- [ ] T-007 (performance regression budget) does not regress.
- [ ] T-008 (fuzz continuous integration) does not regress.
- [ ] T-058 (visual regression for PDFs) does not regress when the bead touches rendering.
- [ ] Bead's parity gate (where it declares one) passes (e.g., T-031 requires 99.9% rule parity vs JVM oracle).

### Bead workflow + multi-agent coordination
- [ ] Agent claimed the bead by `br update <id> --status=in_progress` BEFORE writing code.

### Continuous-integration gates (label `ci`)
- [ ] CI job runs on every pull request and on `main` after merge.

Anything not listed under Acceptance criteria. Future enhancements live in separate beads.

## References

PLAN.md §6 Track 0 T-008.

title: Layered invoice model in Rust (CommercialDocument, ProfileView, JurisdictionExtension, LossinessLedger)
description:
## Background

The invoice data model is the load-bearing decision of the project. Layered: CommercialDocument is the jurisdiction-agnostic core; ProfileView is the projection onto a standard (EN 16931, Peppol BIS, etc.); JurisdictionExtension is polymorphic URN-keyed extension data (NOT a hardcoded struct of country fields); LossinessLedger is the required output of every projection. See PLAN.md §2.2, §4.3.

## Goal

The four layered types exist as `rust_decimal`-backed Rust structs in `crates/ir`, with full unit-test coverage on construction, validation, and round-trip into JSON.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] `CommercialDocument` carries all fields enumerated in PLAN.md §4.3 (id, document_type, issue_date, supplier, customer, lines, monetary_total, etc.).
- [ ] `JurisdictionExtension` is polymorphic: `{urn: String, payload: serde_json::Value}` with per-URN schema lookup at validate-time.
- [ ] `LossinessLedger` is a structured type with fields preserved/lost lists.
- [ ] No hardcoded country structs in the core type — extension data is added by per-country crates at load time via a registry.
- [ ] Unit tests construct each type from synthetic fixtures; round-trip into serde_json::Value succeeds and equals the input.
- [ ] `cargo test -p invoicekit-ir` passes.

## Agent kickoff — read this before you touch any code

If an upstream bead is open, this bead should not be `in_progress` — your claim was premature.
4. **Read the referenced PLAN.md sections.** They contain the architectural context this bead assumes. The PLAN is at `/home/durakovic/projects/invoices/plans/PLAN.md` in this repo.
5. **Reserve files via Agent Mail.** Call `file_reservation_paths(project_key=<abs repo path>, agent_name=<your name>, paths=[<paths you will edit>], ttl_seconds=3600, exclusive=true)`. This prevents a parallel agent from clobbering your work in the same crate. Hold the reservation until you close the bead.
6. **Post your design plan as a comment.** `br comments add <bead-id> --body "Design: <one paragraph on approach, file layout, traits, testing plan>"`. This becomes the contract the reviewer holds you to.

### While you work

- **Commit small, push often.** Open the pull request in DRAFT mode as soon as the first happy-path test passes, not at the end. Reviewers prefer 10 small incremental diffs to one giant one.

### Before you close

- **Open the PR (out of DRAFT), get a review, merge.** CI must be green. At least one reviewer (human or AI) must approve.

Shipped the layered invoice IR: CommercialDocument, ProfileView, JurisdictionExtension {urn,payload}, LossinessLedger, typed validation errors, decimal-string money fields, and JSON round-trip/unit tests in crates/ir. Discovered follow-ups: invoices-c2ow, invoices-qovx, invoices-9cty, invoices-ebaq.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Design: implement the T-010 source-of-truth model inside crates/ir only. Add serde/serde_json/rust_decimal dependencies; define CommercialDocument, ProfileView, JurisdictionExtension, and LossinessLedger plus supporting value types from PLAN.md section 4.3. Monetary values use rust_decimal internally and serialize as fixed decimal strings. Constructors validate required fields, basic date/currency/URN shape, non-empty parties and lines, and keep jurisdiction data polymorphic as {urn, payload} without hardcoded country structs. Tests cover happy-path construction, serde_json round-trip equality against a synthetic fixture, decimal string boundaries, and explicit validation failures for invalid dates/currencies/empty lines/extensions.

title: TypeScript type generation from JSON Schema
description:
## Background

Downstream TypeScript SDK depends on these types. Generated, never hand-written.

## Goal

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] `bunx tsc --noEmit` passes on the generated types.

## Agent kickoff — read this before you touch any code

- [ ] At least one rustdoc example per public function or trait method that actually compiles (verified by `cargo test --doc`).
- [ ] The docs site (T-113) has a corresponding page added or updated where the bead introduces a user-facing capability.

title: `invopop/gobl` bidirectional adapter
description:
## Background

GOBL is the closest OSS neighbor (Apache 2.0, Go). Per §2.9, we interoperate with their JSON schema rather than reinvent it. A bidirectional adapter `gobl <-> CommercialDocument` lets customers move data freely.

## Goal

A bidirectional adapter between InvoiceKit's CommercialDocument and GOBL JSON.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] `to_gobl(doc: &CommercialDocument) -> serde_json::Value` produces GOBL-conformant JSON.
- [ ] `from_gobl(json: &serde_json::Value) -> Result<CommercialDocument>` works for all GOBL document types we cover (invoice, credit_note, debit_note).
- [ ] Round-trip tests: `gobl -> ir -> gobl` is byte-stable for all of GOBL's example invoices.
- [ ] LossinessLedger is populated when fields cannot round-trip cleanly.
- [ ] Tests cover at least 20 invoices from GOBL's own test corpus.

close_reason: Closed by PR #49 — GOBL bidirectional adapter (to_gobl + from_gobl + GoblEnvelope + LossinessLedger). 12 unit tests covering schema URL emission, supplier/customer tax_id and address mapping, line items + totals, bidirectional round-trips for invoice/credit-note/debit-note, error paths, and ledger writes for payee loss + synthesized meta. title: `money` crate (`rust_decimal` based)
description:
## Background

Monetary values never use floating-point arithmetic. The `money` crate is the type that all other crates use for monetary boundary values.

## Goal

A `Money` type backed by `rust_decimal::Decimal` with serde support, currency-code aware, with deterministic rounding policies.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] `Money` is `Decimal + Iso4217Code` with serde via fixed-scale string representation.
- [ ] Operations: add, subtract, multiply by scalar, allocate (Stripe-style banker's allocation), with overflow protection.
- [ ] Property-based tests verify associativity, commutativity, and allocation invariants.
- [ ] Rounding policies (half-up, banker's, half-even) selectable per operation.
- [ ] `cargo test -p invoicekit-money` passes with 100% line coverage.

title: `codelists` crate (signed, versioned, effective-dated)
description:
## Background

Code lists (ISO 3166, ISO 4217, UN/ECE units, VAT category codes, Peppol code lists, country-specific tax category codes) are versioned, effective-dated data — not constants. See §2.3.

## Goal

A `codelists` crate that loads signed, dated code list snapshots and answers lookup queries with effective-date semantics.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] Each code list has a signed manifest with version, effective-from, effective-to, source URL, retrieved-at.
- [ ] API: `lookup(list, code, on_date)` returns `Option<Entry>`.
- [ ] Initial seed includes: ISO 3166-1, ISO 3166-2, ISO 4217 (2024), UN/ECE recommendation 20 units, EN 16931 VAT category codes, Peppol code lists.
- [ ] Update path documented; codelist updater (T-018) lands updates atomically.
- [ ] Property test: lookup on any effective date returns the entry valid on that date.

## Agent kickoff — read this before you touch any code

This section is identical across every bead and exists so any agent (Claude, Codex, or any future binding) follows the same starting protocol. Skipping these steps causes silent collisions with other parallel agents and produces work that does not survive review.

### Before you write code

comments.text: Design: implement T-016 inside crates/tax-calculation as a pure, deterministic decimal arithmetic API layered on invoicekit-money. title: `rulepack` crate (signed manifest format, source registry)
description:
## Background

Rule packs are signed, versioned, effective-dated artifacts. Every validator (T-031, T-030 sidecars) and every country crate consumes rule packs through this crate. See §2.4.

## Goal

A `rulepack` crate that loads, verifies, and serves signed rule pack manifests with effective-date queries.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] Manifest schema includes: source URLs, retrieved-at timestamps, upstream version, effective date range, code list versions, raw upstream checksums, generated metadata, parity fixtures, known gaps.
- [ ] Signed manifests (Sigstore or minisign) verified on load; unsigned packs refused in CI.
- [ ] Effective-date query API: `pack_for(country, profile, on_date)` returns the right pack.
- [ ] Initial registry includes EN 16931 (CEN), Peppol BIS 3.0 (OpenPeppol), XRechnung (KoSIT).
- [ ] Continuous integration refuses unpinned rules.

title: Codelist updater with provenance checksums
description:
## Background

Code lists drift. The updater pulls fresh data from authoritative sources, verifies checksums, and atomically swaps the code list version.

## Goal

A CLI + scheduled job that updates code lists from authoritative sources with full provenance.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] `invoicekit codelist-update --list=iso-4217` fetches, verifies, and writes the new snapshot.
- [ ] Each update is signed and tagged with retrieved-at, source URL, expected checksum.
- [ ] Continuous integration job runs nightly and opens a pull request on diff.

## Agent kickoff — read this before you touch any code

This section is identical across every bead and exists so any agent (Claude, Codex, or any future binding) follows the same starting protocol. Skipping these steps causes silent collisions with other parallel agents and produces work that does not survive review.

### Before you write code

1. **Claim the bead atomically.** Run `br update <bead-id> --claim` to assign yourself as owner and set status to `in_progress`. If the claim fails because the bead is already claimed, do NOT take it — pick another from `bv --robot-next`.
2. **Read this entire bead from top to bottom.** Specifically including the universal quality gates and the applicable type-specific gates below. Make a mental list of which type-specific gates you will need to waive (record the rationale for the close step).
3. **Walk the dependencies.** Every bead under "Dependencies" upstream of this one has shipped what you need; read each upstream bead's body (`br show <id>`) so you know exactly what API contract you can rely on. If an upstream bead is open, this bead should not be `in_progress` — your claim was premature.
4. **Read the referenced PLAN.md sections.** They contain the architectural context this bead assumes. The PLAN is at `/home/durakovic/projects/invoices/plans/PLAN.md` in this repo.
5. **Reserve files via Agent Mail.** Call `file_reservation_paths(project_key=<abs repo path>, agent_name=<your name>, paths=[<paths you will edit>], ttl_seconds=3600, exclusive=true)`. This prevents a parallel agent from clobbering your work in the same crate. Hold the reservation until you close the bead.
6. **Post your design plan as a comment.** `br comments add <bead-id> --body "Design: <one paragraph on approach, file layout, traits, testing plan>"`. This becomes the contract the reviewer holds you to.

### While you work

- **Commit small, push often.** Open the pull request in DRAFT mode as soon as the first happy-path test passes, not at the end. Reviewers prefer 10 small incremental diffs to one giant one.
- **Re-read the acceptance criteria daily.** Universal + type-specific gates are dense; one re-read per day prevents end-of-bead surprise gaps.
- **Post progress comments at meaningful milestones.** "Design locked", "happy path passing", "fuzz harness green", "ready for review". Six comments across a bead's lifetime is good; one comment is too few.
- **If you hit a blocker, post it.** Don't silently switch beads. The blocker becomes a follow-up bead with `discovered-from:<this-bead-id>`.

### Before you close

- **Walk every gate.** Universal section, all applicable type-specific sections, plus this bead's specific gates. Mark each as PASS or WAIVED. A waived gate needs a one-line rationale in the close reason.
- **Open the PR (out of DRAFT), get a review, merge.** CI must be green. At least one reviewer (human or AI) must approve.
- **Close with a complete reason.** `br close <bead-id> --reason "Closed by PR #N — <one-line summary>. Waived gates: <list with rationale>. Discovered follow-ups: <list of new bead IDs>."`
- **Release file reservations** via `release_file_reservations(...)` so the next agent can take adjacent work.

## Universal quality gates (apply to every bead, non-negotiable)

These hold for any bead to be closed. They are ADDITIONAL to the bead-specific gates above, not replacement. A bead with all bead-specific gates met but a single universal gate failed is NOT closed.

### Completeness — no stubs, no half-done work
- [ ] Zero `todo!()`, `unimplemented!()`, `panic!("not implemented")`, or `unreachable!()` left in production paths.
- [ ] No commented-out implementations, no `// TODO` comments left behind, no `XXX`/`FIXME` markers.
- [ ] No placeholder values (no `"REPLACE_ME"`, no `42` as a sentinel, no example secrets in env files).
- [ ] No silent stubs that return `Default::default()` or an empty result when the real implementation is missing.
- [ ] Every code path enumerated in the bead-specific acceptance criteria has been implemented AND exercised by at least one test.

### Tests — happy path + failure modes
- [ ] At least one happy-path test.
- [ ] At least three explicit failure-mode tests (invalid input, downstream failure, edge case).
- [ ] Unit tests cover the bead's public API surface.
- [ ] Integration tests where the bead touches I/O, another bead, or an external service.
- [ ] Property-based tests where the bead has algebraic or canonicalization invariants (use `proptest`).
- [ ] Test coverage on the bead's primary crate / module ≥ 90% line coverage.

### Determinism and reproducibility
- [ ] Where the bead produces output that is signed, hashed, audited, or compared elsewhere (canonicalization, fingerprint, PDF render, ABI surface, signatures), output is byte-identical across two runs on the same input on the same platform.
- [ ] Where the bead targets cross-platform parity, output is byte-identical across Linux x86_64 and macOS aarch64 (Windows x86_64 best-effort).

- [ ] Structured logs using the `tracing` crate. Logs carry `trace_id`, `tenant_id` (where applicable), and `bead_id` (for diagnostic correlation).

- [ ] Library code does NOT panic on user input. Internal invariant violations may use `debug_assert!`.

- [ ] At least one rustdoc example per public function or trait method that actually compiles (verified by `cargo test --doc`).

- [ ] Public-facing copy (CLI help text, error messages, docs) read aloud for clarity.

title: XML canonicalization (C14N 1.1 plus invoice-specific overlay)
description:

## Goal

A pure-Rust XML canonicalizer for our invoice XML outputs, producing byte-identical output across runs and platforms.

close_reason: Closed by PR #14 https://github.com/MuhDur/invoicekit/pull/14 — shipped quick-xml XML canonicalization with invoice namespace overlay, property/cross-platform determinism tests, and 1 MiB Criterion benchmark. Gates: CI green on PR #14; local cargo fmt --all --check, cargo test --workspace, cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings, RUSTDOCFLAGS=-Dwarnings cargo doc --workspace --no-deps, ubs touched files exit 0; XML bench 21.411-22.038 ms under 50 ms target. Waived/N/A: GitHub approving review waived after explicit RainyBeaver review request plus CI-green 60s no-blocker grace window received no response; docs-site/release-notes N/A because this is pre-release internal foundation API; rendering/fuzz/PDF visual gates N/A because no rendering/fuzz surfaces changed. Discovered follow-ups: none.

comments.author: WindyEagle
comments.text: Design: extend invoicekit-canonical with canonicalize_xml alongside the existing RFC 8785 JSON API. Use quick-xml as required by the bead, parse invoice XML as an event stream, omit XML declarations/comments, expand empty elements, sort namespace declarations and attributes deterministically, normalize CR/entity escaping per C14N no-comments behavior, remove inter-element whitespace-only text, and apply an invoice overlay that maps known UBL/CII namespace URIs to stable prefixes such as cac/cbc/ext. Add property tests for idempotence and round-trip stability, deterministic tests for namespace prefix normalization and attribute ordering, plus a tracked criterion bench for a 1 MB XML canonicalization operation in the existing bench harness/budget.

comments.author: durakovic
comments.text: Ready for review: implemented canonicalize_xml in invoicekit-canonical with quick-xml streaming parse, no-comments XML C14N-style escaping/order, UBL/CII prefix overlay, typed XML errors, generated XML round-trip property test, cross-platform byte fixture, and 1 MiB Criterion bench wired into bench.yml/budget.toml. Local gates: cargo fmt --all --check; cargo test -p invoicekit-canonical; cargo test -p invoicekit-bench-harness --benches; cargo bench -p invoicekit-bench-harness --bench xml_canonicalization -- --warm-up-time 1 --measurement-time 3 --sample-size 20 (21.411-22.038 ms); ubs touched files (exit 0); cargo test --workspace; cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings; RUSTDOCFLAGS=-Dwarnings cargo doc --workspace --no-deps.

title: Property-based canonical JSON and XML tests against synthetic IR
description:
## Background

The canonicalizers must survive arbitrary valid IR inputs without crashing or producing variant output.

## Goal

A property-based test harness that exercises both canonicalizers on synthetic IR inputs.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] Uses `proptest` for Rust.
- [ ] Generates synthetic valid IR documents and asserts: canonicalize is idempotent (canon(canon(x)) == canon(x)).
- [ ] Asserts canonicalize is deterministic across runs.
- [ ] Runs 10,000 cases per PR in CI.

## Agent kickoff — read this before you touch any code

This section is identical across every bead and exists so any agent (Claude, Codex, or any future binding) follows the same starting protocol. Skipping these steps causes silent collisions with other parallel agents and produces work that does not survive review.

### Before you write code

1. **Claim the bead atomically.** Run `br update <bead-id> --claim` to assign yourself as owner and set status to `in_progress`. If the claim fails because the bead is already claimed, do NOT take it — pick another from `bv --robot-next`.
2. **Read this entire bead from top to bottom.** Specifically including the universal quality gates and the applicable type-specific gates below. Make a mental list of which type-specific gates you will need to waive (record the rationale for the close step).
3. **Walk the dependencies.** Every bead under "Dependencies" upstream of this one has shipped what you need; read each upstream bead's body (`br show <id>`) so you know exactly what API contract you can rely on. If an upstream bead is open, this bead should not be `in_progress` — your claim was premature.
4. **Read the referenced PLAN.md sections.** They contain the architectural context this bead assumes. The PLAN is at `/home/durakovic/projects/invoices/plans/PLAN.md` in this repo.
5. **Reserve files via Agent Mail.** Call `file_reservation_paths(project_key=<abs repo path>, agent_name=<your name>, paths=[<paths you will edit>], ttl_seconds=3600, exclusive=true)`. This prevents a parallel agent from clobbering your work in the same crate. Hold the reservation until you close the bead.
6. **Post your design plan as a comment.** `br comments add <bead-id> --body "Design: <one paragraph on approach, file layout, traits, testing plan>"`. This becomes the contract the reviewer holds you to.

### While you work

- **Commit small, push often.** Open the pull request in DRAFT mode as soon as the first happy-path test passes, not at the end. Reviewers prefer 10 small incremental diffs to one giant one.
- **Re-read the acceptance criteria daily.** Universal + type-specific gates are dense; one re-read per day prevents end-of-bead surprise gaps.
- **Post progress comments at meaningful milestones.** "Design locked", "happy path passing", "fuzz harness green", "ready for review". Six comments across a bead's lifetime is good; one comment is too few.
- **If you hit a blocker, post it.** Don't silently switch beads. The blocker becomes a follow-up bead with `discovered-from:<this-bead-id>`.

### Before you close

- **Walk every gate.** Universal section, all applicable type-specific sections, plus this bead's specific gates. Mark each as PASS or WAIVED. A waived gate needs a one-line rationale in the close reason.
- **Open the PR (out of DRAFT), get a review, merge.** CI must be green. At least one reviewer (human or AI) must approve.
- **Close with a complete reason.** `br close <bead-id> --reason "Closed by PR #N — <one-line summary>. Waived gates: <list with rationale>. Discovered follow-ups: <list of new bead IDs>."`
- **Release file reservations** via `release_file_reservations(...)` so the next agent can take adjacent work.

## Universal quality gates (apply to every bead, non-negotiable)

These hold for any bead to be closed. They are ADDITIONAL to the bead-specific gates above, not replacement. A bead with all bead-specific gates met but a single universal gate failed is NOT closed.

### Completeness — no stubs, no half-done work
- [ ] Zero `todo!()`, `unimplemented!()`, `panic!("not implemented")`, or `unreachable!()` left in production paths.
- [ ] No commented-out implementations, no `// TODO` comments left behind, no `XXX`/`FIXME` markers.
- [ ] No placeholder values (no `"REPLACE_ME"`, no `42` as a sentinel, no example secrets in env files).
- [ ] No silent stubs that return `Default::default()` or an empty result when the real implementation is missing.
- [ ] Every code path enumerated in the bead-specific acceptance criteria has been implemented AND exercised by at least one test.

### Tests — happy path + failure modes
- [ ] At least one happy-path test.
- [ ] At least three explicit failure-mode tests (invalid input, downstream failure, edge case).
- [ ] Unit tests cover the bead's public API surface.
- [ ] Integration tests where the bead touches I/O, another bead, or an external service.
- [ ] Property-based tests where the bead has algebraic or canonicalization invariants (use `proptest`).
- [ ] Test coverage on the bead's primary crate / module ≥ 90% line coverage.

- [ ] Where the bead produces output that is signed, hashed, audited, or compared elsewhere (canonicalization, fingerprint, PDF render, ABI surface, signatures), output is byte-identical across two runs on the same input on the same platform.
- [ ] Where the bead targets cross-platform parity, output is byte-identical across Linux x86_64 and macOS aarch64 (Windows x86_64 best-effort).

- [ ] Structured logs using the `tracing` crate. Logs carry `trace_id`, `tenant_id` (where applicable), and `bead_id` (for diagnostic correlation).
- [ ] No `println!` / `eprintln!` left in library code.
- [ ] All errors are typed (`thiserror` or equivalent). User-facing errors include a remediation hint.
- [ ] Library code does NOT panic on user input. Internal invariant violations may use `debug_assert!`.

### Documentation
- [ ] Every public item carries rustdoc. `cargo doc --workspace --no-deps` compiles without warnings.
- [ ] At least one rustdoc example per public function or trait method that actually compiles (verified by `cargo test --doc`).
- [ ] The docs site (T-113) has a corresponding page added or updated where the bead introduces a user-facing capability.
- [ ] The changelog / release notes draft updated.

title: Deterministic invoice fingerprint (BLAKE3)
description:
## Background

The reconciliation engine (T-070..T-080) uses a deterministic content-derived ID to dedup invoices. Formula: `blake3(supplier_VAT || customer_VAT || issue_date || document_number || total_amount || currency)`.

## Goal

A function `fingerprint(doc: &CommercialDocument) -> Blake3Hash` plus its test vectors.

## Acceptance criteria (strict gates — bead-specific)

- [ ] Pure function (no I/O, no globals).
- [ ] Property test: same input → same output across runs.
- [ ] Negative test: changing any input field produces a different fingerprint.
- [ ] Test vectors documented and committed.
